reviews.review_description:
This is a really fun game but comparing it to beat saber, it’s very underwhelming. I expected this game to have a longer story mode and have more stuff in freestyle but when I got it, I was disappointed. This game, if given more updates, can become one of the most popular rhythm vr games.￼ 

They can add real life songs like “Somebody I Used Too Know”, “Smooth Criminal”, or “King Of New York”.
Awesome game. Perfect for someone who likes sword fighting, ￼rhythm games and exercise

The soundtrack is an absolute banger that keeps your heart pumping, and the boss battles are both intense and rewarding. The multiple difficulty levels add great replay value, and the overall experience feels polished and satisfying.

I really like this game, the artwork and the music. But there needs to be some common sense adjustments.

I understand the Dev wants to provide variety, but a sword and punching hand together is silly when you have to punch and slash an enemy at the same time. This makes no sense realistically and breaks immersion. You can't use a long range weapon and punch at the same time, that just messes up your sense of distance and timing. Have some enemies that are sword attacks and some that are close range punches, not both together. The worst for me, and what made me quit was one enemy I had to straight punch and slash diagonally at the same time. I mean come on, that's just anatomically impossible for the player to do. 

I recommend the dev maybe contact some martial arts schools that train with weapons to playtest the game, they will give you good feedback on how to improve the mechanics so players aren't injuring themselves trying to do impossible things.

Also, I noticed in the campaign the enemies sometimes blend together, also sometimes the obstacles blend with the enemies at the same time aswell. Very disorienting and you are forced to choose and lose either way.

developer_response.body:
Thanks for the feedback. There is not a sword with fist combination but claws with fist combination and both weapons have the same virual length, meaning that you can use them for the same range. And also there isn't any strike where you have to punch straight and slash diagonally, you always do the same motion by both hands for all double strike - try it next time and you'll see.

For overlapping enemies, there shouldn't be any sequences like that if you mean one enemy behind another. There might be some sequences on hard where it might seem like that because of depth perception when enemies are close together.

I also recommend to join our Discord if you want to talk more about some of your issues.

reviews.review_title: Top 3 rhythm game with cyber esthetics
reviews.review_description:
A highly promising game. It shares some similarities with Beat Saber, Cybrid, and Until You Fall, but stands out thanks to its impressive variety of weapons and combinations, from sabers to axes and hammers. Combat takes place across up to 360° range, encouraging movement and awareness, so a bit more room space can help (though I was able to complete the game in a fairly small area). It's also more physically / fitness intense than Pistol Whip or Cybrid, making it better for fitness.

Compared to other rhythm games, it also features Act bosses. I was truly impressed by the music choices, especially during the boss fights, which gave off vibes reminiscent of major titles like Cyberpunk, Deus Ex, and even NieR: Automata.

I’ve just finished the campaign (3 Acts so far). Skirmish and custom missions are currently in development.

I’m highly excited about future updates, please keep it going!

This game is sooo much FUN. Action filled, rhythmic fights with heart pounding music. in VR mode I really feel like I am the hero in a sci fi action  movie. The mixed reality mode helps me to play it confidently in my small room without worrying to punch walls and furniture. This game is 10/10!

reviews.review_title: Another music game… but is it?
reviews.review_description:
In a world full of monkey clones this tries to punch above its weight and goes after beat saber. 
That’s not a bad thing, because it really tries to nail its own specific vibe. The colors are there, the music is there, the rage moments are there. So for anyone getting into vr, looking at rager or beat saber, It’s easy to see how some would prefer Rager. It’s in your face. The enemies (yes you’re constantly surrounded by them, they come from all places, so you better get a move on) jump at you and you have a split second to act.
This ain’t beat saber boy and it’s not trying to be :D just be aware that if you’re really into beat saber, this game may feel bland. It does not have a decade of content after all.

reviews.review_title: Great job. Recommended for active users
reviews.review_description: I use my Quest almost exclusively for workouts and staying active while traveling. This is a great addition to my list. Great job guys. Please keep the development in progress. I can’t wait to see what more you do with this.

reviews.review_title: Great game only a humble suggestion.
reviews.review_description:
I'm in love with this game 
Great boss battles, good mixed reality, great for exercise.
My only suggestion/request would be:
1. Please add more enemies/obstacles specially if you see your road map
2. Example: an arrow shooter which you have to block and return his arrows. Obstacles: laser beams  you have to jump or crunch to evade

developer_response.body: Thank you very much for your review and suggestions! More enemy types will be added in future updates:)

reviews.review_description: It is a really fun game but if they added multiplayer it would be a lot funnier
Just wish there was more

Rager is a hard hitting rhythm game that delves into the darker heavier electronic/drum and bass genres.

The games Early Access launch comprises of 12 tracks spread over 3 different difficulties.
The journey from easy to hard is challenging, but fair, as you get a hang of the games 360° gameplay.

The game focuses on both attacks and deflects where there is subtly differences in how you deal with them (a swipe or a push forward into the note)
Whilst the dodge notes will have your ducking and weaving like your Neo from the Matrix.....well not quite XD (make sure you have plenty of space before you start!)

Its has a decent amount of content at launch but being an Early Access release, Im expecting more songs to be added over time and thus giving me a reason to keep returning to the title.

For fans of Beatsaber that love the 360° maps and would like one that puts more emphasis on perfect timing (there a marker to help you time each slice/deflect perfectly)....Rager may be just what your looking for.

developer_response.body: Thank you for your feedback! Hopefully we can get your rating up with more content!

reviews.review_description:
Good rhythm attack game, decent weapon's,need more.

The music tracks are pretty good, think there are 12 tracks, so need more added in future updates.

Graphic wise is great and looks like a lot of detail has gone into it.

The game plays well and I thought it was a bit on the slow side for me.

I do recommend this game at the price point.

You might think of this game in the same category of some other slashing musical games, however this one is in a class of its own. Not only are you slashing, you are also blocking and dodging not just from the front, but on your sides leading to feel like an actualy dance of battle.

There were some times that I feel that I should have hit something or blocked, but overall not bad. That could definitely be a skill issue.

Music is not bad, could see this game succeed with custom maps and maybe even enemy types. Give it a shot if you like these combat music games.

reviews.review_title: Neat game that got the heart pumping
reviews.review_description:
Im a big fan of rhythm games. Granted, I often find the music hit or miss. In this case, it was a big miss with a few songs that were great. 

The climactic boss fight at the end of each section also were really fun and rewarding and intense. 

My only kind of gripe was the overuse of tutorials. Its not rocket science. Other than that, fun game.

Rager is a brand new vr rhythm game in a similar vein to synth riders and beat saber where you do certain things in beat with the music. This game is a little different instead of beating blocks or riding rails in synth riders your beating various enemies to the beat of the music while avoiding different obstacles. This is a great new addition to the core vr rhythm games, it is highly recommended to try this game if you enjoy other rhythm games like beat saber or synth riders.

This game is fast, challenging, and a lot of fun. The music matches your movements really well, and the 360° gameplay keeps you busy with blocking, hitting, and dodging. It looks great with its neon style and runs smoothly.

There are several difficulty levels and a short campaign with 12 levels that get harder as you go. It’s also a pretty good workout since you’ll be ducking and moving around a lot. If you don’t have much space, the mixed reality mode helps.

Online leaderboards and regular updates add replay value. If you enjoy rhythm games or fitness-style VR games, this is definitely worth trying.

I found my self thinking this game would be pretty simple and easy when I first jumped in . Later I found this not to be the case . I really enjoyed the the diverse weapon options and what each one did .  I also found my self really enjoying the music . It kept me engaged for longer period time than I thought so . I would recommend if you find your self liking the beat saber style game play.  I would also give yourself ample room as you will use all 360°.  Which I thought to be a nice twist during gameplay .   ￼

reviews.review_title: Has potential to be the next Beat Saber
reviews.review_description:
Everything is is done very well. Almost perfect for VR. I’m loving the Rager universe and descriptive commentary between each round. 
The music and atmosphere ads intensity to the workout. ￼ Some levels have a great buildup that gets me really pumped and excited to play. 
I’m having a lot of fun trying to do better in harder difficulty. 
360 gameplay is a blast and challenging 
The only problem I have is not enough content right now. Can’t wait for more content and maybe licensed DLC

As much as I like the intuitive approach to attack styles in later levels, it still a bit finicky and is far from perfect.
Overall love the music, definitely some head bangers but you cant let it distract you from your goal. 
Looking forward to the track maker being released later on!

Rager is a enjoyable fast paced rhythm/fitness game that makes you feel like a bad @ss.
The controls are very easy to pick up but quite difficult to master (couldn't even do it on easy).
It has beautiful neon style graphics, ran very smoothly on a Quest 3 and did not notice any bugs during my playtime.

There are four difficulty levels invincible(you dont get hurt), easy, medium and hard.
The campaign mode has 12 seperate levels that get harder as you progress each with
there own music and weapons. The campaign is only about 30-45 minutes long and you have
to be quite fit, I doubt I could play much longer than an hour any way. There is a lot of ducking, swaying
and turning (but there are options to turn them off).

The game also has a mixed reality mode, useful if you don't have much space and are worried about
hitting something in the real world.
This game also has online leaderboards which will help with replayability.

I would recommend this to people looking for another fitness type game.

This game is one of the better rhythm games I have tried and it is intense in a fun way. The music closely matches your actions in the game and the element playing it in 360 degrees paired with the different swing types, blocking, and dodging adds to the complexity and skill ceiling of the game in an interesting way that makes it worth investing time to get better at. I would suggest this game to anyone who enjoys rhythm games but wants something that is the next level and new content is being added fairly consistently so I doubt you will run out of things to do.

reviews.review_title: Rhythm, Until You Fall & Beat Saber mix
reviews.review_description:
Received for free for pre-release testing.
It's a good looking rhythm game that gets the heart pumping without feeling like a workout. 
The music is good and the game is on beat. Reminds me of "Until You Fall" in that you have to attack and block with specific movements according to the indicated markers, while being a rhythm game like Beat Saber or others, where you have to be on beat.
I like the combination, and there are boss fights. Overall a visual style that is well executed and works well on the Quest hardware and I really like the art style.
Devs listen to feedback and implement changes quickly, great response.
For me one of the better workout oriented offerings

developer_response.body: Thank you very much for your review and feedback!

reviews.review_title: This game is on a whole other level
reviews.review_description: I love that this game is similar to beat saber but more detailed like enemies coming at you. And i'm glad more rhythm games are coming to the meta store! Better the options for me to see what I feel like playing for a light work out! 😎

Rager is one of the best games I’ve played so far—addictive, dynamic, and thrilling. Its gameplay makes you feel like the protagonist of a cool retro SEGA-style action game. I even use it as a warm-up before my workouts. Brilliant, energetic, and impossible to put down.

Rager has my kind of atmosphere, the kind of sci-fi but still low tech fantasy. Love it.
The gameplay is fluid and the different weapons are a lot of fun to use.
It took a little bit to get used to how to hit properly but after that period it was great!

RAGER  is a mix of rhythm and melee action, glowing cyber arena slicing, blocking and dodging to the beat of driving electronics, and it delivers a vr rush. The weapons (swords, maces, claws, hammer) gives it solid depth for a rhythm-combat game. It’s still in Early Access, so expect some rough edges.

Rager is a fast-paced rhythm slasher game where you fight waves of enemies by blocking, slashing, and counter to the beat of the music. The different levels of the campaign unlock either a new weapon type or a second weapon for your other hand. The final levels of the campaigns end with a unique boss fight with unique attack patterns. What I believe really makes this game shine vs the other rhythm-based games is how the enemies come at you from different directions. I would highly recommend adding this game to your library if you are looking for a good workout. I am looking forward to the free style mode to see what that brings and would love to see some more campaign levels added in the future!

Rager is a super fun twist on the usual VR rhythm games. Instead of watching notes fly in from across the room, this game throws enemies right up in your face in a 360 degree battlefield, so you’ve gotta rely on quick reactions instead of memorizing patterns. It definitely keeps you on edge in a good way.

One thing I really liked is how each level gives you new weapons to mess around with. It keeps the gameplay feeling fresh, and you’re always adjusting your style depending on what you’re using. It’s a nice change from games where you just stick to the same gear the whole time.

The music is great too—high-energy tracks that match the action perfectly and really pull you into the chaos.

If you want a VR rhythm game that’s a bit more intense and hands-on than the usual “see the note, hit the note” formula, Rager is totally worth checking out. It’s fast, it’s stylish, and it keeps you hooked.

Fun yet brief rhythm game with notes of Until You Fall and Beat Saber. Rager incorporates a 360 degree battlefield to pulse pounding beats while giving you a taste of  one and two handed weapons. There are no power ups here nor leveling progression…however, what Rager lacks in RPG/Arcade elements it more than makes up for with the workout it provides and raw potential.

reviews.review_title: One of the best rhythm games!
reviews.review_description:
Colourful, powerful and sweaty! The dubstep soundtrack is great, and gives epicness to every move: you punch, slash, hammer and dodge with many different weapons (and playstyles), against waves of enemies coming from all directions and unique bosses circling around you!
Beware: the harder difficulties are a real workout! 😁

I definitely stepped out of my comfort zone with this game, that's for sure, but honestly, it was worth it. I've already tried Beat Saber, of course, but this game's 360-degree perspective is something else entirely Being attacked from all sides (especially as the difficulty increases) is fantastic, and the music—what can I say? This electro/rock music is thunderous! No worries for beginners, it's Very affordable and easy to learn at first, and then you can gradually increase the difficulty. You do need to have some space at home, otherwise you'll quickly find yourself saying hello to the walls or furniture.But even if there's not much space, it's manageable if you're careful. I'm really looking forward to new maps and maybe even new enemy or weapon types so I can have even more As for replayability, anyone who enjoys rhythm games and slightly aggressive sounds should definitely go for it.

reviews.review_title: Possibly the next big rhythm game
reviews.review_description:
If there's just one thing I can tell You before playing - it's unexpectedly hard, it's OK to drop the difficulty to easy and start from there - it's quite fun there already!

I dont really vibe with beat saber, I do love Pistol Whip though. There's something about the fight that makes it so much better than just cutting through cubes. Rager is exactly that, the thrill of the fight with cutting, smashing, punching, blocking and dodging, all to the rhythm of a great soundtrack. 

It's super satisfying to get good at, and its a great way to get a little workout in while feeling like a bada**.

developer_response.body: Thank you very much for your review!

reviews.review_title: Until you Fall to the Beat
reviews.review_description: This game really reminded me of the Until you Fall fighting style. The difference being you are fighting to the music and turning your body on at 360 axis vice moving through an environment. They have multiple different weapon styles to fight with. Personally, I struggled a little with the blunt style fist weapons, but all other weapons registered hits exactly as I expected them to. Given the title of "Rager" this is kind of self-explanatory, but all the music is a form of heavy metal and is more targeted at someone in the adult age range. Overall I really enjoy the game. If you are looking for something that will get you spinning and swinging to some fast music then this app is for you.
This game gives me so much dopamine. Please add more songs and modes. Pleaseeeeee!

developer_response.body: Thank you very much for your review! We're working on that:)

reviews.review_description:
Full disclosure: I was provided a copy of this game for free to playtest and review.

Bottom Line: 
Neon-Soaked Rhythm Game with captivating visuals/music and a solid foundation makes this an indie gem.

Overview: 
As a fan of many VR rhythm games (especially one in particular), this is a market that is in demand because it only works in VR; it’s fun, and also has the benefit of being a good cardio workout. But even with the demand, it’s tough to stand out from the crowd. Rager pushes a narrative where players are trying to escape the prison of their own digital mind by smashing and slashing their would-be captors. It so happens that the  enemies’ attacks coordinate to a multitude of catchy EDM beats. 

There are multiple weapons, each with their own attack and block patterns (though some can be a little similar). There are also different enemy types, and they attack in different ways and from all angles, which can make this game a little overwhelming for those new to VR (especially at hard difficulty).

Likes: 
Visuals - Beautiful. I think it’s a terrific coincidence that this games’ release coincides with the theatrical release of a certain movie that also shares in this type of cyber-neon aesthetic. It is well-suited to this type of game.

Music - Catchy electronic beats drive the flow of this game and most of the levels do this to great effect.

Voiceovers - The Idris Elba-type voice that narrates the campaign is a good choice.

Critiques:
More Variety - I wouldn’t mind more enemy and weapon types, some of the weapons work very similarly, I know there is also more content incoming so I’m sure this will be a moot point soon. Hoping for the option to face off against enemies in a procedurally-generated battle to keep us on our toes.

Be aware this is a 360-degree game - If you’re new to VR make sure you practice your turning in VR and use your safety barriers or you might strike something with your hand/controller.

Minor Hit Detection Issues - Each update has been improving hit and block detection with certain weapons (on some it already worked great), I also expect this concern to be a moot point soon.

Tracking - On hard difficulty, enemies come at you very quickly, which isn’t inherently a problem (as they should at that difficulty), but some attack from both directions so quickly that the attack/block indicators seem spaced a little too far apart and the only way I get through them is by knowing the pattern. I wished the attack or block icons stood out a little more (which I know can be a challenge in a game this bright/neon).

Final Cut:
There is a solid rhythm foundation here in Rager, the music and visuals capture a world that I want to explore even more. I enjoyed what is on offer and happily give this game 4-stars (with a 5th star pending exploration of new upcoming content). It’s fairly priced and another indie gem from Flat2VR Studios. If you enjoy fun rhythm games then this one is worth checking out (they even created a demo so you can check it out ahead of time)!
This game is really fun and unique. When I played other rhythm games, they felt like they were lacking something, and here it is slash and dodge awesome idea for a rhythm game

Next to other rythm games, this is not one where i can just ahut off and play. It keeps me on my toes to look around and defend myself from different directions with sometimes overwhelming waves of enemies.
Definately a more action rich take on rythm games.
I alao appreciate the decent number of weapons to switch it up, i especially enjoy the 2 handed staff.
I won't say much about the boss, except that its a cool fight :)

developer_response.body: Thank you very much for your review!

reviews.review_title: Finally a new rhythm game worth my time.
reviews.review_description: The visuals and music are fantastic. The gameplay is the type it's so fun and addictive you don't realize you are getting a good cardio session in until it's done.

Rager is a good rhythm game that is mostly edm or dubstep with aesthetically pleasing visuals being set in the future fighting of thr enemies to the rhythm of the song and you have to parry, attack or dodge the enemy and has a select number of levels and each world has their own weapons, like in the first one you would either get a katana or maces and it will change to either be both or mixed but later in the other worlds it just the same variation of the katana or mace just it looks different and mechanics hasn't really changed and wished there could be more weapon types like a cyan coloured bow for example to spice things up.
The bosses on each level use a mix of tribal music mixed with edm and dont really prefer those types of songs. However, I really enjoyed the first boss in the game it was really satisfying to play that level .

reviews.review_title: A really fun twist to a rhythm game!
reviews.review_description: I'm a big fan of rhythm games as a way to burn some calories and this is a great addition to that. The faster paced parts really get your heart rate going and your body moving. Makes you feel like you're in an action movie. Haha.

I'm reviewing this in early access.

I tried being a playtester on this game because it looked like exactly what I was looking for after Until You Fall. A game that gets you hooking into fighting enemies, completing levels, and giving you that "Just one more level..." feeling when you want to quit. But you're actually moving around and working out the entire time. 

It does the workout part better than Until You Fall, actually, since the dodge mechanics are part of the enemies and they really make you dodge around.

Also, I have to admit, I enjoy the Australian accent on the training robot. However, for the devs, it does mispronounce bass (the instrument / sound (which really should be spelled "base")) as bass (the fish) a few times.

developer_response.body: Thank you very much for your review and feedback!

reviews.review_description:
If you have played Beat Saber and heard some of the music from Thumper, it is kind of like a mix of that as a combat rhythm game. Instead of just sabers all the time, there is a slight mix of weapons and how you use them. Slashing swords/daggers and smashing clubs and two handed staves, while being able to block with either and dodge some shields. It has a campaign that actually has boss fights, like none other I have seen before. The wild thing is you need to have some 360 open space around you, as it ramps up from forward facing slashing, smashing, blocking and dodging to suddenly coming at you from all directions. Even on easy, those boss fights really make you work for them. 

The campaign feels kind of short, once you have made it through there. But, then you can challenge yourself on higher difficulties and see how you compare to the global leaderboards. The game is in early access, but the developer has listened to feedback and is still expanding, such as customizations to the weapons. If you like rhythm games and combat games, this is the perfect combo.

developer_response.body: Thank you very much for your review! We will roll out additional content soon.

reviews.review_description:
Rager is easily one of my favorite games to come out this year. The fighting feels so fluid and cinematic. Blocking with one sword and then spinning to cut some dude in half with your other sword is just excellent. Every weapon feels really satisfying, and there is such a perfect selection of them to mess with. 

It is a rhythm combat game. It is very arcade-y. If you like memorizing and mastering complicated techniques, this is a really satisfying grind. By the time you are up and slicing and dodging on hard mode, you can feel the precision and speed from your own body. 

I found this game to be very similar to Until You Fall and Batman Arkham Shadow. It is even pretty similar to beat saber. I've played a lot of all of these games, and Rager is right up there. It is so good, and I recommend it to all of my vr buddies.

developer_response.body: Thank you very much for your review! Much appreciated!

reviews.review_description:
If you like rhythm + fighting genre, then you’ll probably enjoy this game. There are boss levels, and the enemy models make each hit feel impactful and satisfying. It’s a decent workout and tons of fun. 

That being said, the game is still in its early stages and there are some drawbacks. It doesn’t have many options or customization settings. It is very light on content, so it will rely on you wanting to keep replaying the same levels (though you can choose different weapons to give slight variety).

The game is a good and fun workout, and I’m happy to support the potential here, but I would love to see more content and customization in the future.

developer_response.body: Thank you for your feedback! Hopefully we can get your rating up with more content and customization settings!

reviews.review_description: I've been playing this game since beta testing. By combining my favorite BeatSaber, rhythm game and Until You Fall, blade battle game, they created more than that. The effect of cutting out enemies at once makes me constantly challenge myself. Having a brief experience at first, I finished all the levels with normal difficulty. Since I had to swing the attacking enemies from 360 degrees in all directions with the different types of weapons on my left and right hands matching enemy type, I had to show constant tension and quick agility.  I proceeded without difficulty except for repeating it a few times before the boss level in the middle. Hard difficulty is felt like the highest level in beatsaber. At early access, 12 levels seem insufficient, but since it supports level customization, I expect a good level including K-Pop to come out soon.

developer_response.body: Thank you very much for your review! Yes, more content will roll out gradually during EA.

reviews.review_description: This game is a lot of fun and to me kind of breaks the mold on standard rhythm games by adding boss fights, different weapons strikes and attacks. The music and visuals are good and the boss fights are surprisingly fun ( I wasn't ready for 360 but once I got it down it added a new level of complexity. I'd definitely recommend to anyone looking for a unique rhythm action experience.

developer_response.body: Thank you so much for kind words and feedback!

reviews.review_description:
Graphically, the game is good.
The music is excellent too and puts you in the heat of the action!
It can be played by beginners and veterans (no motion sickness).

The EA currently consists of three stages, each divided into four levels, each last with a boss fight.
The content can be completed in less than an hour, but the duration is easily extended by the three progressive difficulty levels (easy/medium/hard) and depending on your desire to advance in the leaderboard.
The roadmap includes the ability to add your own music, which would theoretically lead to an infinite lifespan, but this isn't currently available.

Unlike classics of the genre such as Beat Saber, Pistol Whip, etc... whose levels are built on rails, Rager constructs its static levels where you face waves of enemies in 360 degrees, which drastically changes the approach and requiring more sustained attention.
You play with predefined melee weapons according to the level (sword, hammer, fists, mini-blades, staff, etc.) and whose sensations are quite different from each other. 
It requires you to either attack by performing the movement marked on the screen (slash in a given direction), dodge barriers, or parry enemy attacks (align weapons as indicated). It's quite similar to Until You Fall (instructed motions).
There are no unnatural movements that will require you to make an unnatural gesture, which is worth noting.
The notion of scoring is solely induced by the timing of your strikes/blocks (indicated on the screen by the marker), and the score is largely based on this.

The game has a fairly narrow field of view and the on-screen markers sometimes appear too quickly or too close to the eyes - making it more of a reflex-based action game with simple sequential patterns to know than a pure rhythm game where you have to learn sometimes precise long sequences.
This is probably intentional in the game design especially since the 360-degree construction deconstructs any notion of reference.

Good job !

developer_response.body: Thank you very much for your review!

reviews.review_description:
The moment you go into this game, your jaw drops, it looks amazing, graphically it looks great on the quest 3.
The tutorial coaches you through the early stages, and is seamless, the immersion is on point, it’s a great workout as well as giving a great amount of fun.
The weapons are first class and look stunning.
This is a great game if you want something completely different to other fitness type games.
Well done devs.

developer_response.body: Thank you very much for your review!

reviews.review_description: Rager is an impressive and intense rhythm game that blends awesome music with fast paced weapon based combat. It feels like what would happen if Beat Saber had a baby with Until You Fall. Timing is everything, and the game keeps you constantly moving as you slice, block, and dodge incoming attacks to the beat of the track. With full 360 degree gameplay, you’re never standing still and can even incorporate some dance skills. When playing sometimes I felt like I was Eddy Gordo from Tekken with weapons in hand and others the star wars kid. Visually and musically, the game hits hard. The environments are immersive, the soundtrack is excellent, and it can double as a surprisingly good workout. With it being only early access, you only get 12 killer songs, 3 difficulty levels and 6 different weapon styles but there are other features to come. I had the most fun on medium, but I definitely worked up the most sweat on hard, it really tests your reflexes! Overall, it delivers a fresh twist on the rhythm genre that keeps you coming back for more.

developer_response.body: Thank you very much for your review!

reviews.review_description:
I've always had poor readability capabilities, so I was quite skeptical at how much I would enjoy the
game with its 360 degree gameplay. But in the end I found RAGER to be much more manageable than I
expected, even on the hardest difficulty! Overall providing a solid, satisfying experience I would
recommend to both new and experienced players to this style of game

A few notes I wasn't too sure how to word into paragraphs

* Targets overall are spaced, and distinct between themselves well without having either too small or
too big of a sight picture

* The tutorial is a bit too long for how intuitive the controls are - You can skip it through settings if you
got the gist of it!

* I've had quite a few hit detection issues with blues ones specifically. If I were to guess, it was
because I was too close to it

* More accessibility settings would be nice! I especially found recovering from ducking hard

developer_response.body: Thank you very much for your review and feedback! We will bring more content in and also accessibility settings during EA.

reviews.review_title: This is more a workout game.
reviews.review_description: Great game and quite a workout. Keep in mind this is not a Beat Saber clone, even though it may look like it on the surface. It requires much more movement and reflexes and dexterity than BS. That being said if you are looking for something to train physically with this is the game for you.

developer_response.body: Thank you very much for your review!

reviews.review_description:
This is a rhythm combat game, everyone keeps saying beatsaber but this is nothing like beatsaber imo, if i had to compare it to a type of game i would say similar to "until you fall" only faster pace and a metal like soundtrack. You have either a blunt weapon (smash or punch) and a slice weapon.  Sometimes the weapons are the same on both sides and somerimes you have one of each, the enemies are colored same as the weapon type you slash, smash or block the enemy there are also targets you duck or dodge.
The gameplay is fun but can be chaotic at times especially at the end of each level they seem to come quicker and less time between enemies and on some levels the combat is 360 there are indicators that show where the next enemy is coming from and also there will be some double strikes where you use both weapons on single enemie, there are many different levels some with boss fights, the music is pretty good and it will definitely get your cardio in for the day.  There are many different levels with 3 difficulties. 
What i didn't like, the levels have fixed weapons one will be both slash, the next may be both bash and then you will have slash in one hand and bash in the other. then the next level the weapons will switch hands this is where I had the most difficulty trying to remember which hand has the weapon I need for that enemy and sometimes the double strike with two different weapons is hard to master, but that all becomes easier the more you play.  There are indicators of where you have to slash, bash or block an enemy and you can slash in either direction of the indicator but the bash is done in a specific direction.  
The game is very fun and i would definitely put this in the accidental workout category as this is an active game.

developer_response.body: Thank you for your review! About chaotic level design, you playtested version which wasn't for public and where many things weren't implemented. I recommend to check out the newest version which we released to Early Access - you will be satisfied much more. And about switching weapons - goal of the campaign among other things is to teach player how to fight with different weapons, so that's why you have new weapons for each level. We're currently working on a Freestyle mode which will give you ability select weapons for each level according to your preference, so hopefully this gives you want you want.

reviews.review_description: This game makes you sweat and it's pretty fun the boss will have you smiling being attacked from every angle it's genuinely a very good rhythm/fighting/exercise game

Unlike Beat Saber this has multiple weapons which are swapped and mixed each song so there's different ways to attack notes/enemies. It also has 360° range of motion instead of one direction which is something Beat Saber ditched years ago. This also makes it a lot more physical as you have to dodge and swing in every direction. Best of all the game completely changes once you unlock the dual blade/axe weapon, this could only be achieved in Beat Saber by physically joining your controllers together, this is what separates the game from everything else in the genre. My only gripe is the lack of content, it really needs more songs. Overall a very physical rhythm game that focuses more on variety and a cinematic experience, I'd also consider it an exercise game.

developer_response.body: Thank you for your feedback! Yes, we're already working on more content, which will roll out during early access. If there is something else which could be improvement, let us know. We'd like to try make the best possible experience for everyone. Hopefully, we can get your rating up with more content.

reviews.review_description:
Edit 2: I can see why you urged me to finish the campaign! The final leg definitely stood out far more than the first 2. I look forward to playing that style more when Freestyle releases and you've definitely earned another star from me!

Original review (Edit 1: found the words to describe what felt lacking to me):
Outside of the unique theming, Rager is essentially a clone of Beat Saber. Not necessarily a negative but definitely something to keep in mind.

At first glance, Rager is a slow game. This very much threw me off as someone that prefers fast songs in rhythm games. However, Rager isn't afraid to throw enemies (notes) at you from all directions and that more than makes up for the lack of raw BPM. The weapon variety is also a nice (albeit mostly cosmetic) way to induce different movements as opposed to the wrist flicking of Beat Saber. Interesting to note is that, compared to Beat Saber, those weapons and the combat theming tricked me into a more engaged, whole body movement than I usually do.

I hope custom song support is in the works and I look forward to what's in store for the future but as it stands, it's mostly just a well-executed clone of Beat Saber with some promising ideas that have yet to be fully explored.

developer_response.body: Sorry to hear that RAGER didn't leave a big impression on you. We see that your review came from playtest group and haven't even played through the whole game, so I definitely recommend to play through the full campaign, experience all the boss fights, and try all weapons. Maybe it will change your mind on what this game is really about. If you have some valid criticism and things which you'd like to improve, we'd love to hear that (here on our Discord), as our goal is to try to make the best possible experience and we always listen for player feedback.

reviews.review_title: Rhythm based action for quite a workout!
reviews.review_description:
Rager delivers a thrilling and immersive VR experience that blends rhythm-based combat with high-energy visuals and music. The controls are impressively intuitive, but some mechanics can be tricky to master, especially when instinct clashes with execution!

The game’s 360° movement and fast-paced action can feel a little overwhelming at first—particularly during your first main boss battle, which throws rapid attacks from all directions and requires really quick reflexes. That said, the challenge is addictive, and I found myself replaying it just to crack the pattern and improve my score.

Visually, Rager shines. Enemy designs are varied and well-animated, and the environments are rich and engaging. The soundtrack, while not my usual taste, fits the game’s aesthetic perfectly and adds to the adrenaline rush. However for me, the physical demands (in particular the squats) can be intense over longer sessions, and my limited playspace at home made full 360° movement tricky. Accessibility options like customizable modifiers (like in Beat Saber or Pistol Whip) would also be a welcome addition.

While I wouldn’t recommend Rager to VR newcomers due to its speed and physicality, rhythm game fans with a decent playspace and stamina will find plenty to love.

As someone who loves Beatsaber this is something I thought looked awesome the instant I saw it. Having played it it’s as good as I wanted it to be and very challenging. If you want a rhythm game that will make you feel like your taking on an army with sick beats playing this is your game.

developer_response.body: Thank you very much for your review! It means a lot!

reviews.review_description: This was a blast to check out.  While the difficulty was higher than expected you can quickly get adjusted.  There are 12 different songs set between 3 different stages.  Each stage has it own weapon styles to try out.  The timing was great in my opinion.  As you slash through or smash your opponents you will also need to dodge and block certain mobs.  The boss battles were challenging and really made use of the 360° capabilities of vr.  While the two handed weapons were my favorite to use and made you feel like a action star.

developer_response.body: Thank you very much for your review.

reviews.review_description:
It is time to RAGE! This game is pretty awesome and a lot of fun. It got me sweating from all the movement! I've played "Until You Fall" many times, and this is just like that, but with rhythm-based music.

I noticed a few punches here and there didn't register, but I'm sure the developers will fix this and optimize the game.

In each session, you're given a variety of weapons to use, and you'll rhythm-fight your way to the boss. It's pretty awesome, and I highly recommend it!

developer_response.body: Thank you very much for your review! Yes, there is one edge case when punch might not be registered, but it'll be fixed soon.

reviews.review_description: I very much enjoyed both the music of the matches, and the combat styles that the game makes you go through as you play. Switching between various styles, from one handed weapons, bow staffs, etc, it made for a really engaging experience. I was looking forward to the next challenge just to see what my loadout would be. I did have to dial down the difficulty a step, but that's less on the game and more about my skill up to that mission. This is definitely one of the better rhythm action games I've played recently, and I can fully recommend this game to anyone in VR, new or experienced.

RAGER is one of the best demos I’ve ever played — you can tell instantly it’s a hit in the making. The final release confirms it: this game is a masterpiece. Everything a player could ask for (and more) is here.

From sword combat to powered gloves, melee weapons, and dual-wielding action, every mechanic feels incredibly responsive. Boss fights are intense, the music pumps you up, and every move hits with weight and rhythm. The name RAGER fits perfectly — slicing, dicing, and pounding enemies in sync feels like pure VR euphoria.

There’s a story mode for longer sessions and extra modes when you just want to jump in, RAGE for a few minutes, and flood your brain with dopamine. Honestly, I can’t think of what could make this game better — it’s as close to VR perfection as it gets.

developer_response.body: Thank you so much for your kind words! It means a world!

reviews.review_description: Rager has an intense soundtrack to pump you up and get you in the zone for a workout. The weapons range from swords, maces, axes and knives to even fists for you to cut smash and parry to. Learning each weapon is easy as they are all colour coded. There are 3 different difficulties for you to pick from but regardless of difficulty make sure you have enough space as you will be turning all around the room. Defeating droids, drones and the boss all feels so satisfying. While there aren't many levels they are really replayable. If you're looking to work up a sweat give this game a go

developer_response.body: Thank you for your feedback and review! Campaign already includes 12 levels including 3 boss fights and we're working on more which will roll out during EA. If you've been part of playtest group and hasn't experienced our game after it was released for public, I recommend to check it out. Maybe, you will like it even more!

reviews.review_description:
Rhythm combat fun that hits hard with every beat 
The characters and the bosses level are great  the visuals are fantastic it will definitely give you a workout and if Rhythm games are your go to you need this in your library

developer_response.body: Thank you very much for your review!

reviews.review_description:
I play a *lot* of fitness games, and I do find Rager to be unique enough to stand out from the pack. In the spectrum between "fitness app" (Supernatural, etc) and "gameified fitness", this is very much the latter. In the same way that Pistol Whip turns "gun-fu" ranged combat into a workout, Rager turns melee combat into a fun time that'll have you sweating!

The moveset is actually rather varied, considering the length of the game. The gameplay loop boils down to this: enemies charge and jump at you, accompanied by an icon that dictates how you're supposed to interact with that enemy. There are directional slashes with sword-like weapons, directional smashes with mjolnir-like hammers, blocks, dodges, single-handed weapons and double-handed weapons. It's a fun mix of stuff, and I will say that the double-handed weapons felt quite novel!

In terms of atmosphere, you're always in some form of neon-drenched cyberpunk-esque combat arena. The enemies themselves are repeated, which is fine, but there is also at least one boss fight - it was a cool time, and I won't spoil it.

The playtime/duration is pretty short at the moment - the campaign currently consists of 12 stages/tracks, which I finished on medium difficulty in just over an hour. There are three difficulty levels, and I will say that hard mode definitely tests your reflexes, while easy mode is great for more casual users. There is more content of some form in the works, given that this is just an early access release.

My only real criticism is that, given the game's 360° gameplay, sometimes targets started flying at me from a new direction and I either didn't have enough time to turn before the targets were right in front of me, or the game didn't effectively broadcast that I was supposed to turn (and/or in which direction to turn). I also found a few moments where there was a bit too much gameplay information (targets, etc) happening on the edge of my periferal vision for me to keep track. BUT, again, the title is currently in early access - the whole point is to iron out bugs and balance gameplay like this. I was a playtester for Rager (full disclosure: I did get the game for free as a playtester), and provided this constructive feedback, so hopefully the devs address these small issues with updates!

Overall, Rager is a good time and the price point is reasonable! Some will find it a bit short in its current early-access state, but the included per-level leaderboard definitely adds replay value for people who like to compete against the global board and their friendslist.

developer_response.body: Thank you for review! If you wrote this feedback based on old version which wasn't meant for public, I recommend to try the most current version.

reviews.review_description:
Combining the rhythm genre where you attack items and dodge on the beat, with slicing  enemies similar to the Wii's Skyward Sword combat is a great way to get the player moving around and having fun. 

Rager takes those two concepts and expands them out into full fledged systems that feels very complete for Early Access. The separate difficulties are enjoyable for a more relaxed situation that will get you moving a bit, or franticly flailing about in a frenzy. 

A great soundtrack and musical tracks kept the energy high and timing everything is the key to winning. It's an enjoyable game already, and I can't wait to see where it goes from here.

developer_response.body: Thank you for your feedback! Hopefully we can get your rating up with more content!

reviews.review_description:
I got early access to this game for testing and feedback.

Take a bit of Until You Fall and a lot of Beat Saber, mix them together and what do you have? Something that might be better than either of them. Do you like the directed slashing of Until you Fall, but you're bored with all the non-slashy bits? Do you like Beat Saber, but wonder what all those cubes actually did to evoke such deadly anger? Well this game is all attacking all the time to music and these aren't just cubes you are fighting.

OK, this isn't Until You Fall. It's just got somewhat similar indicators to tell you where to slash, smash, punch, block or dodge. It isn't actually Beat Saber either, but it's in that vein. Lots of games have tried to out beat the saber. A lot fail utterly. Some games mix it up a little, carve their own nitch, are quite worthy, but not actually that different. I think Pistol Whip is the only one that took the rythym formula somewhere new... Until now.

I actual think it's a better concept than either Pistol Whip or Bear Saber. It's more of a rhythm game than Pistol Whip and I like that. The weapon combinations change for each level and it feels more impactful than changing guns. OK, slashing with a sword isn't technically that different than smashing with a mace, but it *feels* different to cut an enemy in two and smash him into next week. While you are vibing to a song, that different feeling is significant.

So why not 5 stars? Pistol Whip and Beat Saber have years of refinement under their belts. More songs, probably better songs, but to be honest I'm not reviewing them. I liked the songs in Rager a lot, but it's not a level playing field. The Rager interface needs a little more improvement, but I don't think it gets in the way of the core game. I can't tell you much about the song charting on harder levels because I hurt my arm (IRL, not game) which limited my movement. Also, while I really like rhythm games, I'm not that skilled at them.

So, I really enjoy the game as it stands now, and I think they are going to get that last star before much longer!

This game is pretty good and unique from other rhythm games. It uses an attacking and deflecting/blocking mechanic for the rhythm that I haven’t seen in any other game(maybe I just haven’t played enough rhythm games lol). The visuals are amazing with the enemies and world looking amazing. The boss fights are super fun and visually appealing. It has a decent amount of content and the music is really good and fits the game perfectly. It uses a 360 world in the sense where you are going to have to be looking all around you which I find fits this game very well and is very fun. There are a variety of different weapons and each feel like a different way to play the game. One thing I wish they would add is a mod manager in the game where you can download and play custom songs in the game without having to do anything special. If they do this it would easily push this game to 5 stars and even right know it is great and with updates will hit that point.

developer_response.body: Thank you for your review! Yes, Level Editor is on our roadmap!

reviews.review_description:
Rager mixes a number of tried and true rhythm game actions, notably slashing, punching and blocking, with stylish combat that reminded me a bit of Against. The combat opponents add some interest so that it's more than just beating up geometric shapes coming at you.
The highlight was the two-handed weapons which combine the slash/punch actions into a two-handed staff that can also block attacks, requiring two-hand positioning to accurately deal with the attacks.
Content is thin at the moment, with a dozen short songs which step through each weapon combo, and some interesting boss fights at the end of each section.
Overall, lots of fun with great dynamic body movement for a good work-out, through 360 turning rhythm combat with a range of weapons. I'm looking forward to new content being added!

developer_response.body: Thank you for your review! Campaign already includes 12 levels with 3 boss fights, but of course much more content will be added during EA.

reviews.review_description:
I received a copy of this for testing, and it has been shaping up surprisingly well so far.  

This game is a rhythm campaign that essentially combines 360-degree Beat Saber with Until You Fall with high-action slashing and bopping enemies as they jump at you.  Probably the most enjoyable part of it for me is the boss fights at the end of each of the three sections that give a fun alternative to humanoid enemies.

I get that it's early access and will have plenty of room to grow, but I am docking it a star for now because of a couple issues I still have.  One is the weapon variety.  It sells itself as having 6 weapons (Sword/mace/axe/hammer/claws/fists) but it really has two (imo) and you could probably argue three--red weapons slash in a line but you can cut any direction while yellow bash in a direction and the potential third is the staff combinations of these.  To me, the difference between these is practically negligible and it's just "red attack" and "yellow attack" to me.  The other big issue I had (that seems to be getting better) is detection.  I was having my hands be randomly spun or moved against my will causing hits to miss but now I really only see it sometimes with the staves not wanting to twist where I want them and it's much less of an issue (but still seems to be there).  I don't know if it's my Quest version or settings but I did change the tracking speed to be max and that could be part of why it's improved.   The other potential issue I have is minor and I haven't had a chance to ask the devs about it yet so I won't worry about it for this review.

I think most people could easily get their money's worth out of this game--especially if you like chasing better scores and leaderboards.  Looking forward to seeing how this game grows.

developer_response.body:
Thank you for your review and feedback!

The main difference for each weapon at the moment is their category (blunt/bladed -> yellow/red) as you specified, but they also differ in motion you have to do with each weapon, for example with claws you have to fight differently than with swords and the same applies for two-handed weapons, etc. So yes, we're currently not doing their differentiation by some other RPG-styled elements, which we might do for different game mode in the future, but they are still different in their fighting style.

About issue with detection, I guess, it will be something with your tracking. You shouldn't experience that. If it persists, I recommend to join our Discord where I'll try to help you troubleshoot it: https://tinyurl.com/rager-discord

reviews.review_description: RAGER delivers a thrilling mix of rhythm and melee combat that feels tailor-made for VR. Each fight pulses with energy as you block, strike, and dodge in sync with its pounding cyberpunk soundtrack. Developed with clear attention to polish, the game’s neon-soaked visuals and responsive combat make every encounter feel cinematic. While still in early access with limited content, what’s here already shows impressive quality, smooth performance, and a unique rhythm-fighter identity that stands out on Quest. However, I found the full 360-degree movement disorienting or difficult to manage.

developer_response.body: Thank you for the review! We're working on a Freestyle game mode where you will be able to play in only 180 degree if you want.

reviews.review_description:
From the moment you step into RAGER VR, you feel the pulse of the world trying to overwhelm you. In hard mode, every swing, block, duck becomes a physical battle — your arms burn, your breathing accelerates, and your heart is locked in combat. It’s more than a rhythm game: it’s a relentless full-body workout disguised as pure adrenaline. The layers of movement — dual-wield strikes, defensive postures, sidesteps — demand both stamina and precision.

But what truly elevates RAGER is how it challenges the mind in lockstep with the body. In its harder settings, you’re not just reacting — you’re anticipating, pattern-recognizing, pivoting your strategy mid-stream. Every confrontation is a brain gym: your reaction times sharpen, your spatial awareness refines, and you learn to juggle multiple streams of sensory input under pressure.

In short: RAGER VR hard mode is a furnace of strength and concentration. It forces you to grow — physically and mentally — or get carved apart. It doesn’t feel like exercise. It is exercise wielded like a weapon — and your brain is its co-pilot.

Absolutely awesomeness🤟

developer_response.body: Thank you very much for your review! It means a lot!

reviews.review_description: Rager feels a bit like until you fall mixed with beat saber.  It's a fast paced rhythm game where your swings and blocks have to be choreographed according to the incoming enemies.  If you like the idea of getting a bit of a workout without feeling like you're working out, this is a quality game for you.  The hits feel punchy, the audio is on point, and there are lots of cool enemies to counter.  There are multiple difficulty levels so even if your reflexes aren't cat-like, you can still get in and enjoy this game.  However, if you want a challenge the higher difficulties will absolutely present just that!

developer_response.body: Thank you for your feedback! If there's something more you would like game to offer (except of more content which we're working on), let us know!

reviews.review_description:
Rager VR fuses immersive combat with to the classic rhythm game formula creating a unique experience.
The dark soundtrack and impressive visuals make you feel like you are fighting in an arena against countless of gladiators who all have a bone to pick with you.
The game keeps it fresh by introducing different weapons that each bring different fighting or dance styles.
The boss fights crank up the pressure, demanding fast reflexes and precise movements.
It is a more than decent workout, and it feels fantastic when you finally earn that sweet, sweet victory after many attempts at perfecting your skills.
Luckily there are different difficulties that offer fun for both newcomers and rhythm-game veterans.
I would love to see more content and songs come to this game over time.
You do need a bit of swinging space as it offers a 360-degree experience.
This is definitely a game I will be returning too from time to time whenever I want to break a sweat and feel like a champ.

developer_response.body: Thank you very much for your review! More content will be added during EA.

reviews.review_description:
This game is amazing. The visual and music qualities are wonderful. Takes working out to a whole new level. Instead of hitting boxes or circles you are fighting enemies. It isn't just about hitting the opponent. You must react with a certain weapon in a specific way. You have to think about your movement since it goes off of dexterity and reflexes. The levels change it up so there is no memorization. You must react quickly and meticulously. You are dodging, slicing and shoving your enemies. 
The music sets the rhythm, which is very helpful. You are always moving since it is 360 degrees of play. There are 3 difficulty modes and each section ends with a boss that has you moving all different ways. Make sure that you have sufficient space to do this game. There is more to come from the Dev's on this game. This is a must play/exercise game to download.

developer_response.body: Thanks for review! We hope that more content will made you revisit your rating!

reviews.review_description:
I initially left a pretty harsh review for this game based off my time with the playtest build, but after playing the latest release, I have to say it’s come a long way. The improvements are very noticeable and makes the experience much more enjoyable.

Nearly all of my issues with the game have been fixed. The gameplay is smoother and fairer. Enemies no longer spawn behind you or out of view, and hit detection feels reliable and even somewhat forgiving, at least on easy and medium difficulties. Enemy waves are timed much better, less overwhelming, and attack sequences feel natural instead of forcing awkward or uncomfortable movements. Even the difficulty is balanced now — challenging but not punishing.

The visuals are clean, and the music fits the rhythm of the action far better than before. Tutorials are less intrusive, so you can jump into the flow without constant interruptions. The boss fight remains the highlight  for me. Its intense and satisfying, and the level has been reworked to feel rewarding rather than frustrating.

The only thing holding it back is accessibility. Height calibration still defaults too short for me, to the point where I don't have to duck under the hazards, and without artificial turning, some 360 levels are tough if you have a smaller playspace.

Overall, the game has taken huge strides since the first build. It’s no longer chaotic and punishing, but polished, fun, and genuinely satisfying. With a few accessibility tweaks, it could easily be a standout VR rhythm experience.

developer_response.body: Thank you for your updated review! Playtest version wasn't for public, it was very early build where were issues which we were planning to solve during last weeks before the EA release. I hope we can get your rating up even more with more content and options during coming months!

reviews.review_description:
A tough combat: slice, dodge, push. All in one game. 

When your weapons changes, you do a tutorial (automatically), so you can practice a bit.

The toughest levels are those where you have to use 2 different weapons. But that makes it more challenging.

 Follow the rhythm and get your score up (and your heartbeat). 

The music is WOW and the graphics are very good.

developer_response.body: Thank you for your feedback! It would be great if you let us know what to improve to get your rating up!

reviews.review_description: If you like rhythm games like beat saber but would like something heavier and with some combat mixed in this is the game for you. I would also say this is a great game to incorporate into your vr fitness if you're into that. You can get some real cardio here while having fun. Great stuff! Check it out.

developer_response.body: Thank you for your feedback! It would be great if you let us know what to improve to get your rating up!

reviews.review_description: If you ever want a more edgy 360 movement rhythm game this game definitely hits the spot.  The music is engaging, combat is solid, the visual art style is like I said an edgy upgrade version of beat saber with physical enemies instead of just color blocks.  It definitely will make you break some sweat.  Looking forward to more sound tracks and level it is definitely a buy if you love rhythm games.

developer_response.body: Thank you very much for your review!

reviews.review_description:
Rager is a Beat Saber styled rythm game with combat elements. The fighting is a great addition to the etablished Beat Saber rythm genre and its perfect if you are interested in a new fitness rythm game.
You use multiple weapons with different mechanics which adds to the depths. If youre into rythm games you should check it out.
